snippet.title: This is Why You Should Study General Immediately After Technician #hamradio #license
snippet.description:
The video explains that about half of the general exam material overlaps with the technician exam, making it more efficient to study them together. Studying for your ham radio license, specifically the general class, right after the technician license makes the material click faster due to the existing foundation. This approach to exam prep is crucial for anyone serious about getting their amateur radio license. Check out our study guide for more help with your exams!

Get the free Ham Radio Beginner Checklist at: https://hamradiolaunchpad.com/checklist

snippet.title: This Cheap HF Antenna Shouldn’t Be This Good…
snippet.description:
Can a £17 telescopic antenna actually work for HF… or is it just another cheap gimmick?

In this video, I take a proper look at a budget telescopic whip antenna, going through what you get in the box, how easy it is to set up, and most importantly — how it performs on the air!

I put it to the test on 20m, running 100W, and the results genuinely surprised me… pulling in solid 58/59 reports into Norway and Italy.

If you’re looking at getting into HF on a budget, this might be a very interesting option.

🎉 GIVEAWAY WINNER ANNOUNCEMENT 🎉 Congratulations to M9XDJ — please email me at Tom@radioforus.co.uk to claim your prize!
Thanks to everyone who entered — more to come!

🎙️ Radio: Yaesu FT710📡 Power: 100W🪫 Setup: Cheap telescopic HF whip antenna📍 Location: Milton Keynes

👉 Subscribe for more approachable amateur radio adventures!

snippet.title: Before You Buy Your Next Ham Radio… Watch This
snippet.description:
Thinking of buying a new ham radio? Stop for a moment.
This video shares expert buying advice from a leading UK amateur radio retailer to help you avoid costly mistakes and buyers’ remorse.

Whether you’re upgrading your HF rig, choosing your first transceiver, or tempted by the latest shiny box, these tips will help you buy with confidence and get better results on the air.

📡 In this video:

* The biggest mistakes radio hams make when buying equipment
* How to choose the right transceiver for your needs
* Why more expensive doesn’t always mean better
* Real-world advice from inside the amateur radio retail world

00:00 Ham Radio Buying Mistakes Intro
01:28 Why Most Radio Hams Overpay (Title Segment)
01:29 Ham Radio Buying Advice From a UK Retail Expert
03:10 The #1 Mistake When Choosing a Transceiver
05:05 Do You Actually Need That Expensive Radio?
07:40 Hidden Costs Nobody Tells New Hams About
09:55 HF, VHF, UHF Buying Decisions Explained
11:20 How to Buy a Ham Radio Without Regret
12:01 Final Thoughts + Buyer Confidence Checklist (Outro)

If you’re into HF, VHF, UHF, DXing, or portable operating (SOTA/POTA), this is essential viewing before you spend a penny.

👍 Like, subscribe, and share with a fellow ham before they make an expensive mistake!

snippet.title: 🎙️ The SWL News Report: New on Shortwave; Rare BBC Interval Signal; FM Shutdowns in France
snippet.description:
Welcome to the April 2026 edition of The SWL DXing News Report, coming to you from Johannesburg, South Africa. This month’s episode is packed with developments across the bands. On shortwave, there are several notable updates — including a possible new Chinese service from Radio Romania International, the end of Japan’s Kyodo News HF fax service, frequency changes from KNLS, and expanded broadcasts from VOA and RFA Korean services.

I look at the (hopefully temporary) shutdown of Cape Pulpit on 729 kHz Medium Wave, powerful transmissions from Roumoules on 1467 kHz, and the ongoing uncertainty around the future of BBC Radio 4 Long Wave on 198 kHz.

On FM, major changes are underway in France as Radio France begins scaling back FM coverage in favour of DAB+. 

I also explore two excellent new resources for DXers: a powerful global radio database with advanced propagation tools, and a live dynamic map of shortwave stations currently on air worldwide.

As always, the episode includes detailed reception reports from listeners across the globe, the greatest number of reception reports yet in my podcast. These come from the UK, Germany, Ukraine, Mexico, the US, Serbia and Finland — covering everything from broadcast stations to utility signals like DSC maritime transmissions, something entirely different to explore.

Sharing reception reports from listeners is one of the main functions of this podcast and I am very thankful for the continued support.

Finally, I take a look at HF propagation conditions for May 2026 and what to expect on the bands.

📡 Whether you're into shortwave, Medium Wave, utility DXing, or just love radio — there’s something here for you.

🌐 Propagation Forecast Websites
🔗 Current conditions: https://hamradiofornontechies.com/current-ham-radio-conditions/
🔗 Australian HF forecast: https://www.sws.bom.gov.au/HF_Systems/6/1
🔗 Global HF conditions: https://www.sws.bom.gov.au/HF_Systems/6/1/1

🧰 DXing Resources Mentioned in the Podcast
🔗 Bob Griffin’s SW & radio database: https://dxtra.com/
🔗 Live shortwave broadcast map: https://share.google/UZrkhs9IjXaQGGEaX

ℹ️ Other Useful Links
📡 Podcast RSS Feed: https://anchor.fm/s/f9ac4be0/podcast/rss
📖 What are SINPO codes? https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SINPO_code

📩 Contact Me
Have a reception report, recording, or radio news tip?
Email: andre9@duck.com

snippet.title: The Best Antenna Isn’t What You Think
snippet.description:
Looking for the best HT radio antenna upgrade for your handheld? In this video, we take a closer look at the Diamond RH519, Diamond RH205, Smiley Tri-Band antenna, Signal Stuff Super Elastic Signal Stick, and the Ed Fong J-Pole antenna—all known for improving range, signal clarity, and overall performance on VHF, UHF, and even 220 MHz bands.
Whether you're into amateur radio (ham radio), GMRS, emergency communications, or just want better performance from your Baofeng, Yaesu, or Kenwood handheld radio, this video breaks down real-world usability, portability, durability, and signal performance.
🔍 In this video:
 • HT antenna comparison (rubber duck vs upgrade antennas)
 • Best portable ham radio antennas for field use
 • Dual-band vs tri-band antenna performance
 • Signal tests and practical range improvements
 • Flexible vs telescoping vs roll-up antennas
 • Which antenna is best for EDC (everyday carry)
📡 Antennas featured:
 • Diamond RH519 (compact dual-band antenna)
 • Diamond RH205 (telescoping long-range antenna)
 • Smiley Tri-Band (144/220/440 MHz performance)
 • Signal Stuff Signal Stick (durable, flexible antenna)

If you're building a go-kit, bugout bag, or upgrading your handheld radio setup, these antennas are some of the best options available.

snippet.title: HF vs VHF vs UHF – Kaunsa Band Beginners Ke Liye Best Hai?
snippet.description:
Ham Radio mein HF, VHF aur UHF bands ka kya difference hai? 🤔
Agar aap beginner ho ya license lene ki planning kar rahe ho, toh sahi frequency band choose karna bahut important hai.
Is video mein hum explain karte hain:
• HF (3–30 MHz) – Long distance DX communication
• VHF (30–300 MHz) – Local repeaters & beginner friendly
• UHF (300 MHz–3 GHz) – Urban & digital modes
Aap jaanoge propagation, antenna size, range, repeaters aur practical use cases.
Galat band choose kiya toh signal nahi jayega! ⚡

snippet.title: Can You Activate POTA Without Calling CQ?

Can you activate a POTA park without calling CQ? In this video, I demonstrate how to successfully activate Parks on the Air by hunting other stations instead of calling CQ. This simple strategy can be especially useful when  the bands are crowded (like on Support Your Parks Weekend), or you just want a different approach to making contacts.

Whether you're a new POTA activator or an experienced operator looking for another technique, this activation shows a practical, real-world way to get your required contacts and put a park on the air.

Topics in this video:
• Activating POTA without calling CQ
• Hunting contacts to complete an activation
• POTA operating strategies during contests

localized.description:
Can you activate a POTA park without calling CQ? In this video, I demonstrate how to successfully activate Parks on the Air by hunting other stations instead of calling CQ. This simple strategy can be especially useful when  the bands are crowded (like on Support Your Parks Weekend), or you just want a different approach to making contacts.

Whether you're a new POTA activator or an experienced operator looking for another technique, this activation shows a practical, real-world way to get your required contacts and put a park on the air.

Topics in this video:
• Activating POTA without calling CQ
• Hunting contacts to complete an activation
• POTA operating strategies during contests
• Tips for new Parks on the Air activators

snippet.title: The CaHR-Tenna Hydra Multi-Band HF Antenna
snippet.description:
Looking for one antenna that covers multiple HF bands? In this video, I break down a Hydra-style multi-port antenna system, from concept to field use, so you can decide if it’s worth buying.

What you’ll see:

-How the multi-port design works electrically 
-Build approach and hardware layout
-SWR and on-air performance across bands

Were going to show how and why we designed the antenna the way we did with real measurements and deployment scenarios. If you operate portable and want a flexible station setup, this will give you a clear, no-nonsense approach.

snippet.description:
The new PicoBNC+ NVH Diagnostics Kit makes getting started with NVH testing faster and easier.

In this video, Barney explains how licensing works with the new PicoBNC+ probes inside Pico Diagnostics software.

See how simply connecting a PicoBNC+ NVH probe automatically enables NVH functionality, helping reduce setup friction and get you testing faster.

This video also covers:

✔ How automatic recognition works
✔ What happens with unlicensed scopes
✔ Existing BNC NVH kit compatibility
✔ Using previously licensed scopes
✔ Plug-and-play setup with PicoBNC+ probes

Whether you're upgrading from an older NVH kit or using Pico Diagnostics for the first time, this guide explains everything you need to know.

snippet.title: My POTA Activation Was RUINED by This Noise… (Help Me Diagnose It)
snippet.description:
Ever had a POTA activation completely fall apart because of noise you couldn’t explain?

In this video, I head out to US-1972 – Little Miami State Park for a Parks on the Air activation, but things go sideways fast. Instead of a smooth activation, I’m dealing with constant feedback, interference, and noise that makes it nearly impossible to make contacts.

I’ll walk you through my POTA setup, show you exactly what I was hearing on the radio, and break down the possible causes—from RF interference to grounding issues and everything in between.

👉 This is where I need your help.
If you’ve experienced something like this before, drop a comment and let me know what you think caused it.

Whether you're into ham radio, POTA activations, portable HF setups, or troubleshooting weird RF issues, this one’s a real-world learning experience.

snippet.title: The Three-Digit Code Every Ham Operator Uses #hamradio #learning
snippet.description:
RST is the three-digit signal report every ham radio operator exchanges. R is Readability — how clear is the voice? S is Signal Strength — how strong is the signal? T is Tone — mostly for Morse code, less important for voice. 59 means perfect readability, maximum signal. 33 means readable but struggling. "59, good copy OM!" Once you know RST, every contact makes more sense. You'll hear it in every mode — voice, FT-8, CW, all of it. Learn it once, hear it everywhere.

Get the free Ham Radio Beginner Checklist at: https://hamradiolaunchpad.com/checklist

snippet.title: Мы на передовой, стыдно: Туапсе. Ужасные последствия прилетов: город, море в мазуте. Репортаж
snippet.description:
Нефтяное пятно из Туапсе дошло до соседних прибрежных поселков

Жители поселков Тюменский (20 км от Туапсе) и Новомихайловский (40 км) публикуют видео покрытых мазутом пляжей.

«В официальной новостной ленте — георгиевские ленточки, бодрые отчеты и вранье о том, что загрязнение носит локальный характер. За сегодня зона загрязнения в разы увеличилась»,
 — пишут местные жители в группе ВК «Мой Туапсе».

Люди жалуются, что в поселках никто даже не начинал работы по уборке нефтепродуктов.

«Волонтеры и неравнодушные жители краснодарского края организовали пункт по очистке бездомных животных от нефти 

Жители и волонтеры стихийно организовали центр, где отмывают бездомных кошек и собак. Пункт работает уже третий день. Сюда привозят ежедневно десятки животных, которых находят в Туапсе.

Животных отлавливают по всему городу: этим занимаются специальные волонтеры, которых называют «ловцы», они ездят по городу и забирают измазанных в нефти собак. За один выезд им удается поймать до пятнадцати собак. С кошками сложнее, их поймать не так просто, поэтому их меньше. 

«Те животные, которых не смогут отловить волонтеры, у них просто нет шансов», 
— сказала одна из волонтеров нашему корреспонденту.

«Корвалол мой друг на эти дни ближайшие, потому что я видела все эти тонны мазута, которые оставались у меня на перчатке. Это только от одного кота. С одного кота мы смыли, мне кажется, 100-200 мл мазута»,
 — добавила волонтер.  

Вся работа организована исключительно на добровольных началах: неравнодушные жители привозят сюда медикаменты, корм для животных, защитную одежду и еду для волонтеров. Информация о деятельности центра распространяется в основном через чаты в WhatsApp и в мессенджере «Макс».

localized.title: Мы на передовой, стыдно: Туапсе. Ужасные последствия прилетов: город, море в мазуте. Репортаж
localized.description:
Нефтяное пятно из Туапсе дошло до соседних прибрежных поселков

Жители поселков Тюменский (20 км от Туапсе) и Новомихайловский (40 км) публикуют видео покрытых мазутом пляжей.

«В официальной новостной ленте — георгиевские ленточки, бодрые отчеты и вранье о том, что загрязнение носит локальный характер. За сегодня зона загрязнения в разы увеличилась»,
 — пишут местные жители в группе ВК «Мой Туапсе».

Люди жалуются, что в поселках никто даже не начинал работы по уборке нефтепродуктов.

«Волонтеры и неравнодушные жители краснодарского края организовали пункт по очистке бездомных животных от нефти 

Жители и волонтеры стихийно организовали центр, где отмывают бездомных кошек и собак. Пункт работает уже третий день. Сюда привозят ежедневно десятки животных, которых находят в Туапсе.

Животных отлавливают по всему городу: этим занимаются специальные волонтеры, которых называют «ловцы», они ездят по городу и забирают измазанных в нефти собак. За один выезд им удается поймать до пятнадцати собак. С кошками сложнее, их поймать не так просто, поэтому их меньше. 

«Те животные, которых не смогут отловить волонтеры, у них просто нет шансов», 
— сказала одна из волонтеров нашему корреспонденту.

«Корвалол мой друг на эти дни ближайшие, потому что я видела все эти тонны мазута, которые оставались у меня на перчатке. Это только от одного кота. С одного кота мы смыли, мне кажется, 100-200 мл мазута»,
 — добавила волонтер.  

Вся работа организована исключительно на добровольных началах: неравнодушные жители привозят сюда медикаменты, корм для животных, защитную одежду и еду для волонтеров. Информация о деятельности центра распространяется в основном через чаты в WhatsApp и в мессенджере «Макс».

snippet.title: Septuagenarian gets machining certification, sets sights on university｜Taiwan News
snippet.description:
Bilingual text here: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026501W08EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description
雙語文字版: https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/news/2026501W08EA?utm_source=youtube&utm_medium=description

Our official website: ➔ https://english.ftvnews.com.tw/

Follow us on
Facebook: ➔ https://www.facebook.com/ftvformosanews
X (Twitter): ➔ https://x.com/FTV_Taiwan_News
The continuing education department at National Caotun Vocational Senior High School boasts a gold-star student who also happens to be in his golden years! 73-year-old Tsai Chia-tsai not only comes out top of his class every semester, but also spent a year of rigorous study to earn Class B certification as a machining technician. He said he wants to be an example for his grandson and is working hard to achieve his next dream: getting into university.

A man adjusts a machine with intense focus, looking like he has decades of experience behind him—maybe a senior instructor or a master technician.

Scratch that, this young man is actually the instructor! 73-year-old Tsai Chia-tsai is a student in the continuing education department at National Caotun Vocational Senior High. After missing out on an education when he was young, he started taking night classes at 60. At 70, he enrolled in this mechanics program. He’s not only top of his class every semester, but after passing Class C machining technician certification in his second year, he studied hard for a year to attain the difficult Class B level.

Tsai Chia-tsai
Machining technician
The hardest part of the exam was the theory. There are 1,000 questions for that part, including multiple-choice questions that might have more than one answer. Memorizing it all was exhausting. But I want to show young folks and my classmates that we old people can do it too.

While his physical stamina and eyesight may be in decline, Tsai’s resolve is unshakable. His teachers are all impressed, and work hard along with him.

Chen Shih-pin
Instructor
He’s currently 73. In my 10-plus years of teaching, I’ve never seen someone of his age attain Class B certification. He might be the only one in the entire country to pass at his age. 

You’re never too old to learn. Tsai said his greatest motivation is his grandson, who recently started the mechanics program at a Taichung high school. As the “upperclassman" in the family, he wants to set a good example.

Tsai Chia-tsai
Machining technician
When I found out I passed, I almost cried. Seriously. I’m not joking. My grandson said he wants to keep at it, since he’s planning to take the Grade C exam this year.

Hand trembling as he shows his license, this senior student is taking that same passion and roaring headfirst into his next challenge: getting into university! 

For more Taiwan news, tune in:
Mon to Fri at 9:30 pm on Channel 152
Tue to Sat at 1 am on Channel 53

snippet.title: Tips for Activating Parks On The Air: Do You Need Banners and Flags? 📡🌳
snippet.description:
Are you getting ready for your first Parks On The Air (POTA) activation? One of the most common questions beginners have is how much "flair" they should bring along. Should you fly a POTA flag? Do you need a banner to show what you're doing?

In today’s video, we’re diving into the "Low Profile" approach to POTA and why keeping it simple is often your best bet for a successful and hassle-free activation.

📝 The Short Answer: Less is More

The short answer is no. There is absolutely no requirement to create attention for yourself. In fact, looking like a highly organized group with professional signage and massive banners can actually work against you.

Why stay low profile?

Avoiding Permits: Many parks require a "Special Use Permit" for organized groups or events. If you look like a sanctioned festival or a large-scale demonstration, you might be asked to pack up or provide paperwork you don't have.

Reduced Distractions: The more attention you draw, the more people will stop to ask questions. While we love promoting the hobby, sometimes you just want to get those 10 contacts and enjoy the nature!

No Reporting Required: You aren't required to "check-in" with a ranger or report your presence to activate.

🤝 A Note on Courtesy

While there is no legal requirement to notify park officials, it is often a nice courtesy to let a ranger or the front office know what you are doing. A simple "I'm a ham radio operator doing a portable demonstration" goes a long way in building good relationships between the hobby and the Parks department.

✅ Don't Forget to:

Subscribe for more portable radio tips!

Like this video if it helped you plan your next activation.

Comment below: Do you prefer to fly a flag or keep it stealth?

snippet.title: Most Hams Run 100W, These Guys Use 5W #hamradio #QRP
snippet.description:
QRP means running low power — typically 5 watts or less. That's tiny compared to the 100 watts most HF radios put out. And here's the wild part: with the right antenna and good band conditions, 5 watts can talk around the world. QRP operators wear it as a badge of honor. Less power, more skill. The challenge is the point. QRP is ham radio distilled to its purest form.

Get the free Ham Radio Beginner Checklist at: https://hamradiolaunchpad.com/checklist
